findings.title: No healthchecks configured for MongoDB or Redis
findings.description: Neither the mongodb nor redis service defines a healthcheck. Without healthchecks, `docker compose up -d` reports both containers as 'started' as soon as the process launches, not when the service is actually ready to accept connections. The backend connecting before MongoDB finishes its initialization (warm-up can take 2-5 seconds on first start) will receive connection refused errors that are confusing to debug.
findings.impact: Developers running `docker compose up -d` immediately followed by `./gradlew bootRun` may see transient connection errors on first start. nc -z passes as soon as the port opens, not when MongoDB/Redis is ready to process commands. Acceptable for local dev, but degrades onboarding experience on first run.
findings.recommendation:
Add healthchecks to both services:

mongodb:
  healthcheck:
    test: ["CMD", "mongosh", "--eval", "db.adminCommand('ping')"]
    interval: 5s
    timeout: 3s
    retries: 5
    start_period: 10s

redis:
  healthcheck:
    test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
    interval: 5s
    timeout: 3s
    retries: 5

findings.title: No restart policy configured
findings.description: Neither service specifies a `restart` policy. Docker's default is `no`, meaning if a container crashes (e.g., OOM, unexpected exit), it stays down until the developer manually runs `docker compose up -d` again. For local development databases this often causes silent data loss confusion — the developer continues coding while queries silently fail.
findings.impact: If MongoDB or Redis exits unexpectedly during a development session, the container stays down. The backend will log connection errors but the cause (stopped container) is not immediately obvious.
findings.recommendation:
Add `restart: unless-stopped` to both services. This is the standard local-dev restart policy — containers restart automatically on crash but stop gracefully with `docker compose down`:

mongodb:
  restart: unless-stopped

redis:
  restart: unless-stopped

findings.title: No memory resource limits configured
findings.description: Neither service defines `deploy.resources.limits.memory`. MongoDB and Redis both default to consuming all available host memory under load. On developer machines with constrained RAM (e.g., 8 GB with IntelliJ + Node + JVM all running), unconstrained MongoDB can cause system-level OOM events affecting other running services.
findings.impact: On low-memory developer machines, MongoDB's WiredTiger cache (defaults to 50% of available RAM) can starve the JVM and Node processes. Redis is lighter but can grow unbounded with no maxmemory set.
findings.recommendation:
Add memory limits appropriate for local dev. These are conservative starting values that can be adjusted per machine:

mongodb:
  deploy:
    resources:
      limits:
        memory: 512m

redis:
  deploy:
    resources:
      limits:
        memory: 128m

Alternatively, add `command: --wiredTigerCacheSizeGB 0.25` to the mongodb service to cap WiredTiger's cache at the compose level without needing deploy.resources.
